insight: In base set only, P2 responds to natural Animus (P1) with TDD (Tarsier-Drone-Drone) rather than TTD. A Reddit BSO tree comment claims P1 wins if P2 deviates to TTD, though the exact result is debated. Attacking for 2 into Wall is considered a poor response.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Discussion of base-set P2 responses to natural Animus and the BSO tree.

insight: Natural Animus (P1) can be punished by P2 playing a high-econ line: DD/DD/DDE/DA/DDD/Sym + Drone / DDDB / Phage+Tarsier / Wall+X / double Phage / X / Rhino+Blast, giving P2 a sizeable advantage. The first Phage comes online on the last turn so P2 can acquire the Blastforge without wasting red mana.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Pikachumemes arguing that natural Animus is exploitable if P2 plays correctly instead of attacking for 2 into Wall.

insight: Doomed Drone is worse than regular Drone at growing economy toward infinity, based on recursive inflation calculations. A hypothetical 1-gold (no energy) build-time-5 Drone sits between the two in infinite-econ growth rate: Drone ≈ 1.28, bt5-Drone ≈ 1.26, Doomed Drone ≈ 1.228. Without an energy cost, the bt5-Drone is better than Wild Drone for infinite econ (≈1.3247), which silentslayers describes as the best drone for infinite econ.
author: silentslayers
context: Extended theoretical discussion comparing Drone, Doomed Drone, and a hypothetical build-time-5 Drone for pure economic growth rate toward infinity, with Wolfram Alpha calculations linked.

insight: Doomed Drone supply is highly relevant because players want their Doomed Drones to be 'premium' ones available for soak later. The primary value of Doomed Drone over regular Drone is supply and late-game soak utility, not purely economic output, since Drone is better than Doomed Drone for infinite econ growth.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion of when to buy Doomed Drone vs regular Drone, with silentslayers emphasizing the strategic value of preserving Doomed Drone supply for late-game soak.

insight: A hypothetical 1-gold (no energy) build-time-5 Drone is worth buying primarily when you would otherwise float gold and only need economic drone investment (no tech needed). In slow sets with 4th or 5th Engineer, where inflating is desirable but buying tech is bad, such a unit would be bought frequently. If it costs even 1 energy, it becomes unplayable for economic purposes.
author: silentslayers
context: Silentslayers clarifying real-game application of the bt5-Drone thought experiment, particularly in slow sets.

insight: The price difference between Drone and Doomed Drone is 1 gold, and the effect difference is that 5 turns after being built you retain a regular Drone. Therefore, buying a Doomed Drone plus a hypothetical 1-gold no-energy bt5-Drone is essentially equivalent to buying a regular Drone, assuming you never get block value from the Doomed Drone.
author: awaclus
context: awaclus clarifying the mathematical equivalence underlying the bt5-Drone thought experiment.

insight: Early Doomed Drone purchases carry medium-term economic risk: having your economy crater in rounds 5–8 is devastating during the attack race. Converting (getting soak value) can offset the rebuy cost, but failing to convert can be fatal. The rebuy cost becomes unaffordable precisely when the attack race heats up.
author: kevinb9n
context: kevinb9n reflecting on base+Doomed Drone games against Master Bot and revising the Doomed Drone wiki page.

insight: When 'thresholds' (large one-turn purchases like Defense Grid or Colossus) are important, inflation is actually high—one attack this turn is worth more than two next turn—implying it is not a slow game, and players should not be buying 20+ Drones from supply in such sets.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Discussion about the relationship between game speed, thresholds, inflation, and optimal Drone count.

insight: The optimal Drone-to-Engineer ratio for pure economic growth is irrational (analogous to the golden ratio) and can be computed via fixed-point calculations. With 0.28 Engineers per Drone, buying 0.28 Drones per turn costs 0.84 gold and 0.08 Engineers, maintaining a constant ratio without floating gold or energy. This generalizes to Vivid Drone + Drone + Engineer and to Thorium Dynamo sets.
author: aa01blue
context: aa01blue and silentslayers discussing fixed-point math for optimal economic ratios in abstract Prismata economy calculations.

insight: Vivid Drone's perceived strength in real games comes largely from starting with 6–7 physical drones to sacrifice, not purely from the unit's own economic growth rate. If you must buy Drones in order to then buy Vivids (scaling from scratch), the efficiency drops significantly. Wild Drone beats Vivid Drone in pure infinite-econ growth rate calculations.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers correcting the common perception that Vivid Drone is the best economic drone by distinguishing real-game starting advantages from abstract growth rates.

insight: Buying two Drones then a Vivid Drone the following turn (dd this turn → Vivid next) has the same efficiency as buying three Wild Drones. This makes Wild Drone underrated, especially since Wild Drones bought on turn 2 start generating exploitable value by turn 6+.
author: pikachumemes.
context: pikachumemes comparing Vivid Drone and Wild Drone efficiency in real game openings.

insight: Master Bot opened natural Blastforge 'for no reason at all' in a base+1 set where Animo (Amporilla) was available, which pikachumemes considered a clear mistake. This suggests MB makes suboptimal opening decisions in sets where the correct response to an opponent's opening is not straightforward.
author: pikachumemes.
context: holyfire shared a game replay and pikachumemes analyzed it, noting MB's inexplicable natural Blastforge opening.

insight: Amporilla is considered unviable in base+1 sets unless the opponent is going or attempting to go high-economy (or there is a red sink available). Getting a 3-attack Amporilla while sacrificing drone defense and wasting a second Animus due to inability to spend red is a losing line.
author: pikachumemes.
context: pikachumemes reviewing holyfire's loss and explaining why going for Amporilla in a base+1 set was incorrect.

insight: In Plasmafier/Cluster Bolt sets, P2 has a natural Conduit advantage, making it very difficult for P1 to win. If P2 additionally buys Manticore while P1 does not, P2 gains absorb denial on top of the inherent positional advantage, making a P1 win extremely unlikely.
author: pikachumemes.
context: spiritfryer asked why buying more Cluster Bolts (all of which volleyed with Plasma) still led to a loss; pikachumemes pointed to natural Conduit advantage and Manticore.

insight: In a set with Doomed Mech and Feral Hound, a strong P2 line is to rush Steel Forge as fast as possible without floating red, then settle into buying Doomed Mech and Feral Hound each turn while floating 1 red. Ensuring the Feral buy only needs to defend 2 (not 3) creates a gambit versus Doomed Mech hold, combining Tarsier pre-absorb attack, anti-Doomed Mech gambit, and early blocking from Feral buys.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer outlining a solid P2 line in a Steel Forge / Doomed Mech / Feral Hound set.

insight: When racing to be first to Zemora Voidbringer, the first Zemora volley can be very difficult to defend against when combined with units like Nivo, Cluster Bolt, or Venge Cannon, sometimes making it worth cutting economy to get there first. However, a slower Zemora with more economy can win via the counter-volley, so the correct approach requires set-specific judgment.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion about whether P2 should sometimes race to the first Zemora Voidbringer even at the cost of economy.

insight: Vivid Drone and Conduit have roughly equivalent value as snipe targets: Vivid Drone provides +3 gold per turn while Conduit provides approximately +1 attack +1 gold on average, making them about equal in value. This means losing a Vivid Drone to a snipe is not critically more damaging than losing a Conduit.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Discussion about Zemora Voidbringer snipe targets and whether Vivid Drone being sniped is a big setback.

insight: Blood Phage is generally superior to buying Tarsier+Drone when you would otherwise be making that purchase, up to approximately two Blood Phages. Beyond two, Tarsier+Drone is preferable because Tarsier+Drone gives a late-game Forcefield-able Drone that provides blocking value Blood Phage cannot.
author: consensus
context: Thread discussing when Blood Phage is strictly superior to Tarsier+Drone, with wibbie, silentslayers, and kevinb9n all contributing.

insight: When floating 1 gold with no economic edge between Blood Phage and Tarsier+Drone, silentslayers prefers Tarsier+Drone because a single block late in the game can be decisive—especially in Centurion games where 1 defense is 'quite clearly invaluable.' In Wall-absorb games the block matters less but is still non-zero.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers refining the Blood Phage vs Tarsier+Drone decision for edge cases when floating 1 gold.

insight: Going for a 3rd Engineer as P1 in sets containing Vivid Drone, Apollo, or Pixie can be a trap: the temptation toward high-econ openings leaves P1 vulnerable to Bloodrager, which punishes the slower early game.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Discussion of whether players more commonly lose by going 3 engineers or 2 engineers in the wrong set; pikachumemes cited a recent ranked game where opponent's 3rd Engineer as P1 was punished by Bloodrager.

insight: High-absorb units like Energy Matrix do not make attackers weaker; they make it bad to push attack out slowly. Therefore, if you can push attack out quickly without needing many Drones (e.g., Arms Race + Ferritin + Energy Matrix, or Flaming Animus + Perforator), a 2-Engineer opener can be correct even with high-absorb available.
author: megasupp
context: megasupp and spiritfryer discussing 2-Engineer viability in high-absorb sets, specifically base+Energy Matrix and Flaming Animus+Perforator.

insight: In the 'Extremely Unfair' challenge scenario, Master Bot sometimes stops absorbing entirely despite having defense available, apparently because it detects frontline units (Wild Drones) and decides absorbing is suboptimal. This causes it to give up a +2 absorb-per-turn advantage to the player, and it can be exploited by achieving tempo before the bot switches to its Polywall-buying routine.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer writing a detailed walkthrough for the 'Extremely Unfair' bot challenge, discovering the AI stops absorbing due to frontline unit logic.

insight: In the 'Extremely Unfair' challenge (opponent has 1 extra Drone as P2), the AI consistently opens with an early Blastforge on 2 Engineers. Exploiting this by opening Turn 3 Animus, buying 1 Auric Drone instead of 2 Drones to keep turns flexible, delaying your own Forge by one turn, using an early Rhino for defense, and then pumping Tarsiers—eventually buying out all 10—is a winning line against this bot behavior.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer's solution diary for the 'Extremely Unfair' combat scenario challenge, identifying consistent AI opening behavior and the line that exploits it.

insight: For the 'Unfair 2' challenge (opponent has +1 Engineer and Vivid Drone available), one winning line is the 'Elyot Animus' approach: buy 4 Vivid Drones as fast as possible, avoid wasting tech, avoid early Rhino (which gets abused by Militia), and eventually build blue for Iceblades against enemy Grimbotch.
author: pikachumemes.
context: pikachumemes sharing an alternative winning line for the 'Unfair 2' challenge, contrasting with spiritfryer's Apollo/Infusion Grid solution.
